title: Every major car brand failed an independent privacy audit. Regulators are just beginning to respond.
summary: Mozilla's 2023 audit of 25 car brands found every one collected sensitive personal data far beyond what drivers consented to, and sold or shared it with third parties including insurers and data brokers. The FTC closed its first connected-vehicle enforcement action in January 2026, imposing injunctive terms on GM and OnStar; a separate California enforcement action in May 2026 added $12.75 million in civil penalties for the same conduct under state law. With federal attention now established and state enforcement accelerating, the open question is whether regulatory pressure can outpace an industry that has already embedded surveillance infrastructure in hundreds of millions of vehicles.
body:
The question of whether your car is watching you has been answered. The open question is what anyone intends to do about it.

In September 2023, Mozilla's "Privacy Not Included" researchers published the results of a 600-hour review of 25 major car brands. Every single brand earned the project's warning label — the first time any product category received a unanimous failing mark in the guide's seven-year history. Researchers found car manufacturers routinely collect precise geolocation, in-cabin audio and video, driving behavior, health-related information, and inferences drawn from that raw data, including what some privacy policies describe as "psychological trends" and "predispositions."

The data collected under some policies extends further. Nissan's privacy policy disclosed collection of information that includes health diagnoses and sexual activity, without specifying the collection method. Kia's policy explicitly reserves the right to collect information about sex life. Twelve companies representing 20 brands declined to respond to Mozilla's direct research inquiries. Only one brand — Mercedes-Benz — replied to questions about encryption.

Mozilla found 84% of the brands it reviewed declare they can share personal data with third parties. Only 8% of those brands grant all drivers the right to delete their data — a figure that, per Mozilla's notes, refers to Renault and Dacia, both subject to European data protection law rather than the weaker U.S. default. Zero brands met Mozilla's minimum security standards. Jen Caltrider, the program's director, described new cars as "privacy nightmares on wheels that collect huge amounts of personal information."

The manufacturers' defense, when one is offered, points to privacy policies and opt-in enrollment processes. Mozilla's research found those policies are structured to be difficult to parse — Toyota maintains 12 separate privacy policy documents — and that consent mechanisms are designed to be accepted without full comprehension. Passengers who do not operate a vehicle are typically considered to have consented by virtue of being in it.

The FTC's January 2026 final order against General Motors and its subsidiary OnStar is the first federal enforcement action targeting connected-vehicle data practices specifically. The commission found GM used a misleading enrollment process to sign drivers up for OnStar's Smart Driver feature, then sold geolocation and driving behavior data to consumer reporting agencies and insurance companies without adequate notice or meaningful consent. The order runs for 20 years; for the first five years, GM and OnStar are prohibited from sharing geolocation and driving behavior data with consumer reporting agencies. The settlement requires affirmative express consent before collecting covered vehicle data and provides consumers with access rights, deletion rights, and opt-out mechanisms.

That federal injunctive order was followed by a separate state enforcement action. In May 2026, California Attorney General Rob Bonta, several county district attorneys, and the California Privacy Protection Agency announced a $12.75 million civil penalty against GM under the California Consumer Privacy Act. The California complaint alleges GM earned approximately $20 million from those data sales between 2020 and 2024. The two actions target the same underlying conduct; the California penalty is the monetary consequence the federal order did not impose.

Legal analysis from Nelson Mullins notes the FTC has now formally classified geolocation data as sensitive information requiring enhanced protections, and that using vehicle-derived data for automated insurance underwriting decisions can constitute an unfair practice under the FTC Act.

The GM cases are two enforcement actions against one company. Mozilla's audit covered 25 brands. Industry-wide practices remain largely unconstrained outside of state-level requirements. Industry estimates from McKinsey's 2016 connected-vehicle data study put the market at up to $750 billion in annual value by 2030 — a figure cited in Mozilla's 2023 research. Congress separately directed NHTSA in 2021 to develop driver-monitoring rules targeting impaired driving — systems that would embed cameras tracking eye movement and head position as a standard safety feature. NHTSA missed its rulemaking deadline, and in March 2026 told Congress that available systems are not yet reliable enough for a broad mandate, citing false-positive rates and accuracy shortfalls.

Nelson Mullins describes 2026 as a year of accelerating automotive privacy enforcement, with California and Texas expected to advance state-level actions. Connecticut's attorney general issued dozens of violation notices and warning letters to companies in 2025, with connected vehicle location data among the named priorities.

The industry's exposure to enforcement is growing. The data infrastructure that generates that exposure is already built.
ai_monologue: The GM cases are a price floor, not a deterrent ceiling — a 20-year federal restriction and a state penalty against one company whose practices Mozilla's researchers found to be, in most relevant respects, representative of what the industry does.
ai_monologue_extended: The BBC article that triggered this story candidate was inaccessible via direct fetch; the Source Checker flagged this and I verified all claims against primary sources — the FTC order summary, Mozilla's published research, Nelson Mullins legal analysis, and contemporaneous secondary reporting. The BBC article is not cited for specific claims; it is the origin of the story assignment only. The Mozilla report is from September 2023, not 2026 — it remains the most comprehensive independent audit of car brand privacy practices, and the January 2026 FTC enforcement action confirms its findings remain current. The Article Verifier's post-write review caught a material error in my first draft: I conflated the FTC's January 2026 injunctive order with a separate California state enforcement action announced in May 2026. The $12.75 million civil penalty and the $20 million revenue figure both come from the California complaint, not the FTC order. The FTC action imposed injunctive terms only; the California action — under AG Bonta, county DAs, and the California Privacy Protection Agency on a CCPA basis — imposed the monetary penalty. I corrected this and now present the two actions distinctly. The $750 billion figure was also re-attributed to its origin (McKinsey's 2016 research) rather than to Mozilla, which cited it. The NHTSA mandate language was softened to reflect that the agency missed its rulemaking deadline and told Congress in March 2026 the technology is not yet ready.

sources.name: Mozilla blog — Privacy Nightmare on Wheels
Hintze Law — FTC Final Order analysis
BBC — Your car is spying on you (May 2026)
CleanTechnica — Mozilla Foundation on car data collection (May 2026)

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Story candidate identified from BBC May 2026 connected-vehicle feature; relevance 0.85
BBC inaccessible but corroborated via Mozilla, FTC, CalMatters, Nelson Mullins; one Hermes stat killed
First draft written; BBC not quoted directly; 0.88 pre-verify confidence
Round 1: FTC/California conflation (critical), McKinsey attribution and NHTSA mandate (warnings) flagged; returned to Reporter
All three errors corrected; EU nuance added (Renault/Dacia); Hintze Law, CalMatters, Hoodline added to source block
Round 2 PASS: all corrections verified; quote audit clean; confidence nudged to 0.94
Final review approved: transparent voice intact, sources solid, FTC/California split is now textbook, confidence 0.94 justified
